reasoning: Q6 already beats DuckDB (10ms vs 19ms), but Learner identified genuine inefficiencies with high ROI potential. SIMD code falls back to scalar after predicate evaluation (defeats vectorization), madvise hints mismatch sparse access pattern, and 64 threads may cause excessive overhead. With 2 iterations left and low-risk optimizations available, aggressively pursue multiple improvements simultaneously to push 2-4x beyond current performance.
focus_areas: Fix SIMD aggregation to eliminate scalar fallback - compute and accumulate in vector registers (1.5-2x gain)
Optimize madvise to MADV_RANDOM/WILLNEED for zone-map-pruned sparse access pattern (10-20% gain)
Reduce thread count from 64 to 8-16 to minimize scheduling overhead for selective query (5-15% gain)
Add prefetching for next blocks to pipeline I/O with computation on HDD (10-20% gain)
Use aligned SIMD loads (_mm256_load_si256) instead of unaligned for 2x memory throughput (5-10% gain)
strategy_notes: BE AGGRESSIVE. We're already winning but have clear paths to 2-4x further improvement. The Query Optimizer should apply ALL five optimizations together: (1) Rewrite SIMD section (lines 169-211) to use masked SIMD loads/multiplies and horizontal reduction instead of scalar fallback loop; (2) Change madvise from MADV_SEQUENTIAL to MADV_RANDOM or targeted MADV_WILLNEED for blocks passing zone map filter; (3) Reduce thread count to 8-16 (test both) and consider increasing block size to 200K-500K rows; (4) Add __builtin_prefetch or MADV_WILLNEED for next N blocks in worker loop; (5) Align column data to 32-byte boundaries and switch to aligned loads. These target different subsystems (CPU/SIMD, I/O, threading) so they compose well. Expected combined outcome: 3-6ms (3-6x faster than DuckDB). If any single optimization regresses, rollback system will catch it.
notes: All selected optimizations are low-to-medium risk with concrete implementation paths. Since we're already ahead of benchmark, we can afford to be aggressive and learn what works. The optimizations are orthogonal (SIMD compute, I/O pattern, thread management) so minimal interference expected.
